coredata.dc:title: Maillard reaction for nucleation of polymer quantum dots from chitosan-glucose conjugate: Antagonistic for cancer and viral diseases
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmed, Hanan B.
El-Shahat, Mahmoud
Allayeh, Abdou K.
Emam, Hossam E.
coredata.dc:description: Polymer dots (PDs) ingrained from biopolymers are characterized by their biocompatibility & non-toxicity to be superiorly applicable for biomedicines. The point of novelty in the current study is to focus on the effect of Maillard reaction for conjugation of chitosan with glucose to enhance the affinity of chitosan as a biological resource of PDs. Chitosan-glucose conjugate was firstly prepared by Maillard reaction. PDs were nucleated from chitosan (C1 acidic, 10.6 nm & C2 basic, 11.4 nm) and chitosan-glucose conjugate (C3 acidic, 6.8 nm & C4 basic, 5.7 nm). The affinity of chitosan versus chitosan-glucose conjugate as molecular precursors for PDs as antiviral and anticancer laborers was studied. The synthesized PDs were tested against lung cancer (NSCLC, A549) and the estimated IC50 was 282.4 & 165.4 μg/mL for PDs (C3 & C4) ingrained from chitosan-glucose conjugate. The antiviral action of PDs against Coronavirus (229E) was estimated and the obtained IC50 for C3 & C4 was 43.6 and 19.3 mg/mL, respectively. PDs ingrained from chitosan-glucose conjugate showed higher anticancer and antiviral activities compared to that clustered from chitosan. Consequently, the modification of chitosan via Maillard reaction enhanced the biological affinity of the obtained PDs to be effectively applicable as antitumor and antiviral laborers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer dots
Maillard reaction
Chitosan-glucose conjugate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay and anticancer potency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation scheme and mechanism of the chitosan-glucose conjugate.
FTIR spectral data for the target molecules.
NMR spectral data for the chitosan-glucose conjugate; [a] 1HNMR spectra and [b] 13CNMR.
TEM micrograph and size distribution for the synthesized PDs samples;[a] G1, [b] G2, [c] C1, [d] C2, [e] C3 and [f] C4.
[a, b] Emission spectra (excitation wavelength = 370 nm) at room temperature for the synthesized PDs samples and [c] Photographic images of solutions under UV-lamp (365 nm).
FTIR spectral data for the synthesized PDs samples.
1HNMR Spectral data for the synthesized PDs samples; [a] C1, [b] C3 and [c] C4.
13CNMRSpectral data for the synthesized PDs samples; [a] C1, [b] C3 and [c] C4.
Anticancer activity for the synthesized PDs; [a, b, c] cell viability and [d] IC50 values.
Antiviral activity for the synthesized PDs; [a, b, c] cell viability and [d] IC50 & CC50 values.

head.ce:title: Maillard reaction for nucleation of polymer quantum dots from chitosan-glucose conjugate: Antagonistic for cancer and viral diseases

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Helwan University, Ain-Helwan, Cairo 11795, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Helwan University, Ain-Helwan, Cairo 11795, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photochemistry Department, Chemical Industries Research Institute, National Research Centre, Scopus affiliation ID 60014618, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Giza 12622, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Water Pollution Research Department (Virology lab 176), Environment and Climate Change Institute, National Research Centre, Scopus affiliation ID 60014618, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Giza 12622, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Water Pollution Research Department (Virology lab 176), Environment and Climate Change Institute, National Research Centre, Scopus affiliation ID 60014618, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Giza 12622, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pretreatment and Finishing of Cellulosic based Textiles, Textile Research and Technology Institute, National Research Centre, Scopus affiliation ID 60014618, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Giza 12622, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pretreatment and Finishing of Cellulosic based Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pretreatment and Finishing of Cellulosic based Textiles, Textile Industries Research Division, National Research Centre, Scopus affiliation ID 60014618, 33 EL Buhouth St., Dokki, Giza 12622, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer dots (PDs) ingrained from biopolymers are characterized by their biocompatibility & non-toxicity to be superiorly applicable for biomedicines. The point of novelty in the current study is to focus on the effect of Maillard reaction for conjugation of chitosan with glucose to enhance the affinity of chitosan as a biological resource of PDs. Chitosan-glucose conjugate was firstly prepared by Maillard reaction. PDs were nucleated from chitosan (C1 acidic, 10.6 nm & C2 basic, 11.4 nm) and chitosan-glucose conjugate (C3 acidic, 6.8 nm & C4 basic, 5.7 nm). The affinity of chitosan versus chitosan-glucose conjugate as molecular precursors for PDs as antiviral and anticancer laborers was studied. The synthesized PDs were tested against lung cancer (NSCLC, A549) and the estimated IC50 was 282.4 & 165.4 μg/mL for PDs (C3 & C4) ingrained from chitosan-glucose conjugate. The antiviral action of PDs against Coronavirus (229E) was estimated and the obtained IC50 for C3 & C4 was 43.6 and 19.3 mg/mL, respectively. PDs ingrained from chitosan-glucose conjugate showed higher anticancer and antiviral activities compared to that clustered from chitosan. Consequently, the modification of chitosan via Maillard reaction enhanced the biological affinity of the obtained PDs to be effectively applicable as antitumor and antiviral laborers.

ce:section.ce:para: Abundance of novel synthetic methodologies for carbon dots (CDs) results in their convenient and effective applications [1]. Carbon dots are nucleated via top-down methodology, that depends on fragmentation of carbon allotropes like nanotubes, graphene and fullerenes, or via bottom-up methodology whereas carbon hydrates are used as carbon molecular precursors to interact with the solvent under certain conditions [2]. The synthetic methodologies like hydrothermal/solvothermal [1] and microwave-assisted techniques [3] are the straightforward and the most common ones, that comprise no additional or fewer chemicals. The other non-common techniques, that exhibit reasonable results, are chemical ablation, electrochemical carbonization and laser ablation [1]. The as-mentioned methodologies produce CDs with various applications, that explaining the versatility of carbon dots [4]. According to their composition, CDs are categorized into three types [2]; carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polymer dots (PDs) [2,5]. All types of carbon dots are characterized with superior optical properties despite their dissimilar composition, surface functional groups and size.

GQDs are composed of graphene layers with characteristic functional groups on the surface, that are originated from carbon sources in the reactants [2]. CNDs are spherical in shape and could exhibit an amorphous structure to be expressed as carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) or graphite-like crystalline lattice to be known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [2]. On the other hand, PDs could exhibit either polymer chains aggregated around a spherical carbon core or cross-linked/aggregated linear polymers [2]. Therefore, the optical properties of these nanostructures were shown with different emission wavelengths, intensities and different lifetimes depending on their chemical composition, size and surface functional groups. However, the optical properties of the various types for carbon dots could not be exploited for their differentiation [6]. Ananthanarayanan and coworkers were systematically studied the relationship between morphological features and applicability [6], as they estimated the wavelength of emission for GQDs depended on the shape, size and the surface functional groups, whereas, their report showed an increment in wavelength with size and with the number of surface functional groups [6]. Considerably, the coworkers also reported that various types of doping with heteroatoms can motivate either a blue shift or a red-shift on the emission spectral results. Most importantly, depending on the type of protecting or stabilizing agent exploitable for passivation, carbon dots could exhibit negligible cytotoxic effects, making it suitable for in vivo experiments [7]. Hitherto also considered with studying various types of carbon dots, most of which were characteristic with broad absorption peaks in ultraviolet-visible spectra and photoluminescence emission bands in the visual spectra from blue to green region [8]. Even though many reports were elucidated the exact mechanism for interoperating the superior properties of carbon dots, none of the reported mechanisms was affirmed. The most acceptable mechanism so far supposes that the photoluminescence emission originates from the surface functional groups on carbon dots edges and not from their particle size, as quantum dots originated from metal salts [6]. Specifically, the absorption of carbon dots in ultraviolet-visible spectra, exhibited with a band at 230–280 nm, that is referred to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 carbon‑carbon bonds. Zheng et al. reported that, a shoulder between 300 and 550 nm is ascribed for n-π* transition of CO/CN and CO/CN bonds or other connected groups [5], where graphene quantum dots with adjustable particle size were supposed to be linked for shifting in the maximum of the emission spectra. Accordingly, we can summarize that, although the quantum confinement is not the only reason for the optical properties of carbon dots, it acts a critical role in the absorption spectra and emission and has to be considered when ascribing the characters of these unique structures.

On the other hand, chitosan, as a biopolymer composed of D-glucosamine and N-acetyl glucosamine units and originated from chitin, deserves special consideration owing to its biological characters like its non-toxicity, biocompatibility and biodegradability [9,10]. Chitosan with superior characteristics, such as low molecular weight, reducible viscosity and enhanced antioxidant and antimicrobial potentialities, could be obtained via depolymerization of chitosan with physical, chemical or enzymatic hydrolysis methodologies. Such characteristic properties of chitosan depend on its molecular weight, degree of acetylation, distribution of acetyl groups and viscosity [11–13].

Li et al., were reported that, there are two different cross-linking browning methodologies, identified as caramelization and Maillard reaction (MR), whereas, caramelization is proceeded via immediate heating of polysaccharide, while Maillard reaction (MR) proceeds via the condensation reaction between the nitrogen-containing reactant and the carbonyl group of a reducing sugars [14]. Esuriently, heating is a physical technique that exhibited observable effects on the enhancement of thermal stability, water solubility and mechanical & barrier properties [15–17]. However, MR is a chemical procedure that involves three steps through which the initial products are identified as shift bases that via rearrangement reaction could form Amadori products, which undergoing further reactions to produce irreversible advanced glycation end products [13,18]. This process produces fluorescent, brown MR products with improved chemical, sensory, antioxidizing and microbicidal properties of chitosan [19].

It is a fascinating challenge to synthesize polymer dots (PDs) clustered from biopolymer with biological activities, to be exploitable as antiviral and anticancer laborers. The current approach considered with systematical studying for the affinity of chitosan-glucose conjugate as a molecular precursor in clustering PDs to be concurrently applicable as antiviral and anticancer reagent. In the presented work, chitosan-glucose conjugate was generated as a product of MR, to be subsequently exploited as a molecular precursor for nucleation of PDs with cytotoxic effects against both of lung cancer cells and Low Pathogenic Corona Virus (229E). The nucleated PDs were prepared under hydrothermal conditions and dialyzed for obtaining highly purified/monodispersed PDs. Successive synthesis of PDs via exploitation of chitosan-glucose conjugate was approved via different instrumental analyses like, UV–Visible spectroscopy, Transmission Electron Microscopy, FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. Consequently, the photoluminescence properties of the prepared PDs were studied. The anticancer potentiality of the prepared PDs was approved via Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay for following up the cell mortality effects against lung cancer cells. Additionally, the viricidal activity of the prepared PDs was affirmed via CPE-inhibition Assay, while, CC50 & IC50 were also estimated.

Chitosan (85 % degree of deacetylated and molecular weight of 150 kDa) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. d -Glucose, methanol; acetic acid, 2-propanol and DMF were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Company. All materials were used without further purification. De-ionized water was used in all experimental processes. Pur-A-lyzer dialysis kits (MWCO 6–8 kDa, from Sigma-Aldrich) was applied for dialysis. Molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) is expressed for pore size. Dalton (Da) is an alternate name for the atomic mass unit, and kilodalton (kDa) is 1000 Da. Thus, a molecule with a mass of 8 kDa has a molecular weight of 8000 g per mole.

Chitosan (150 mg) was dissolved in 20 mL of (1.5 %, v/v) acetic acid solution and it was stirred at 60 °C for 3 h to obtain homogeneous clear solution. The solution of d -Glucose (162 mg, 0.9 mmol) in 10 mL of methanol was added, followed by stirring at 60 °C for 2 h. The reaction mixture was allowed to cool and keep it to dry under room temperature. After solvent evaporation takes a place, the dried material washed with DMF to remove all un-reacted d-Glucose and dried under vacuum at 85 °C for 24 h.

10 g/L of glucose, chitosan and the prepared chitosan-glucose conjugate were hydrolyzed separately under both of alkaline (20 g/L of NaOH) or acidic (20 g/L of acetic acid) conditions to be subsequently left under magnetic stirring for 30 min at 100 °C. Afterwards, under hydrothermal conditions for clustering of PDs, in vertical hydrothermal autoclave reactor, the obtained colloidal solution was transferred and left in oven for 6 h. in the current approach, the hydrothermal technique reaction was proceeded at 180 °C, to ensure the complete carbonization of molecular precursor with avoiding the decomposition of the capping reagent (chitosan macromolecules) as it might result in a bad surface passivation, losing (or deterioration) of the surface functional groups. Eventually, the obtained PDs colloid with dark brown color was left for cooling in the atmospheric air to be subsequently dialyzed with distilled water for ultra-purification to produce ultrafiltered/monodispersed/purified PDs for further analyses [20].

Investigation of the chemical composition of chitosan-glucose conjugate as a condensation product from the interaction of glucose with the exploited polysaccharide (chitosan) and the sequential ingrained PDs were confirmed via the different spectral mapping data. Infrared spectral analysis was performed by using Jasco FT/IR 6100 spectrometer, whereas the PDs colloidal samples were formerly dried at 110 °C and grinded in a hammer to be ready for characterization using FTIR. Absorbance spectral data were estimated at 500–4000 cm−1 using 15 points smoothing, 4 cm−1 resolution, 64 scanning times with scanning rate of 2 mm. sec −1. Additionally, NMR spectra were determined on a Jeol-Ex-300 NMR spectrometer (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) in deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO‑d 6) at sample concentration of 20 mg/mL and stirred for 24 h at 70 °C and chemical shifts were expressed as part per million; (δ values, ppm) against TMS as an internal reference, National Research Center, Cairo, Egypt. 1H and 13C chemical shifts were referred to the solvent signal (DMSO- d
6 ) at 2.50 and 39.52 ppm, respectively. Data are presented as follows: chemical shift, integration, multiplicity (br = broad, s = singlet, d = doublet, t = triplet, q = quartet, m = multiplet) and coupling constants in Hertz (Hz).

Photoluminescence of PDs in ultraviolet-visible range were analyzed via Spectro-fluorometer (JASCO FP8300). Spectral analyses were performed at room temperature with excitation at 340 nm, while, emission was detected at room temperature. Size distribution and topographical features of the clustered PDs were estimated via anticipation of High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope from Japan (JEOL-JEM-1200). Size distribution of PDs was evaluated by 4 pi analysis software (from USA) for at least 25 particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay and anticancer potency
ce:section.ce:para: Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay is considered as one of the most widely applicable methodology for in vitro cytotoxicity screening [21]. The assay depends on the affinity of SRB for chemical bonding to the protein components of the tested cancer cells (human non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) cell line, A549) that were fixed to the tissue culture plates by trichloroacetic acid (TCA). SRB is bright-pink amino-xanthene dye with two sulfonic groups that chemically bonded to the basic amino-acid moieties under a mild acidic condition, and decompose under a basic condition [22]. As the chemical bonding of SRB is stoichiometric, the content of the dye extracted from the stained cells is directly proportional to the cell mass. The sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay is applied for the cell density detection, based on the estimation of the cellular protein content. The methodology ascribed to be optimized for the toxicity screening of components to adherent cells in a 96-well format. After incubation the examined cells that were formerly exposed to the currently prepared PDs colloid, the cell monolayers are fixed with 10 % (wt/vol) trichloroacetic acid to be stained for 30 min, afterwards the excess dye is eliminated with repeated washing cycles using 1 % (vol/vol) acetic acid. Protein-bonded dye is dissolved in 10 mM Tris base solution at 510 nm via a microplate reader. The data are linear over a 20-fold range of cell numbers [23].

The CPE-inhibition assay will be used to identify the potential antiviral activity of the clustered PDs against Low pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) according to literature [23]. The dose-response assay was designed to determine the range of efficacy for the chosen antiviral, i.e., the 50 % inhibitory concentration (IC50), as well as the range of cytotoxicity (CC50). This assay is critical for determining antiviral efficacy in cell culture systems. Nawah-Scientific, Egypt, provided low pathogenic Coronavirus (229E) and Vero E6 cells. Vero E6 cells were grown in DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 0.1 % antibiotic/antimycotic solution. Gibco BRL provided the antibiotic & antimycotic solution, trypsin-EDTA, fetal bovine serum, and DMEM medium (Grand Island, NY, USA). The Crystal violet method was used to evaluate antiviral activity and cytotoxicity assays using the recently reported for the cytopathic (CPE) inhibition effect. In brief, Vero E6 cells were seeded into a 96-well culture plate at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well one day before infection. The culture medium was removed the next day, and the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline.

The infectivity of Low Pathogenic Corona Virus (229E) was determined using the crystal violet method, which monitored CPE and allowed the percentage of cell viability to be calculated. 0.1 mL of diluted virus suspension of 229E containing CCID50 (1.0 × 106) of virus stock was added to mammalian cells. This dose was selected to produce the desired CPEs three days after infection. For compound treatments, 0.01 mL of medium containing the desired compound concentration was added to the cells. The antiviral activity of each sample was determined using a 10-fold diluted concentration range of 0.1–1000 μg/ml. The virus controls (virus-infected, nondrug-treated cells) and cell controls (non-infected, nondrug treated cells). For 72 h, culture plates were incubated at 37 °C in 5 % CO2. The development of cytopathic effect was monitored by light microscopy. Following a PBS wash, the cell monolayers were fixed and stained with a 0.03 % crystal violet solution in 2 % ethanol and 10 % formalin. After washing and drying the optical density of individual wells was quantified spectrophotometrically at 570/630 nm.

The percentage of antiviral activity of the currently clustered PDs was estimated via Pauwels et al. (1988) using the following equation: antiviral activity = [(mean optical density of cell controls − mean optical density of virus controls) / (optical density of test − mean optical density of virus controls)] × 100 %. Based on these results, the 50 % CPE inhibitory dose (IC50) was calculated. Before this assay, we assessed the cytotoxicity; cells were seeded at a density of 2 × 104 cells/well in a 96-well culture plate. The next day, the culture medium containing serially diluted samples was added to the cells and incubated for 72 h before being removed and the cells washed with PBS. The following steps were carried out in the same manner as described above for the antiviral activity assay. The results of 50 % cytotoxic concentrations (CC50) and the 50 % inhibitory concentration (IC50) were determined using GraphPad PRISM software (Graph-Pad Software, San Diego, USA).

In accordance to literature [24,25] and as suggested in Fig. 1 , herein, glucose as a reducible monosaccharide with carbonyl groups and chitosan as nitrogen containing polysaccharide was exploited for preparation of chitosan-glucose conjugate via Maillard reaction. Whereas, the current work is considered with an innovative synthetic route for nucleation of PDs with diversifying chitosan-glucose conjugate as molecular precursor that was preliminary produced via Maillard reaction. Maillard reaction is well known for synthesis of chitosan conjugates but the point of novelty in the current approach is the exploitation of such produced conjugate as molecular precursor for PDs. As schematically represented in Fig. 1, with exploitation of chitosan-glucose conjugate as molecular precursor, controllable clustering of PDs was carried out under both of acidic and basic conditions via hydrothermal technique. The mechanism of PDs nucleation could be postulated as follows; firstly, under both of alkaline and acidic conditions with mechanical stirring, chitosan-glucose conjugate moieties were supposed to undergo condensation reaction with the release of water to form an oxygen bridge [26,27]. Afterwards, successive clustering of PDs from the condensed chitosan-glucose conjugate moieties was proceeded via two main steps; carbonization and surface passivation (surface functionalization). In carbonization the organic molecular precursor (chitosan-glucose conjugate) is converted into their basic carbon nano-structural moiety that was currently performed under hydrothermal conditions. Passivation as the next step is required in order to minimize the effect of the surface-defect, trap sites, and the direct quenching from the surrounding media, for sequential improving of the photoemission. Whereas, the carbon nano-objects were not usually exhibited with optical properties, therefore, chitosan as nitrogen containing biopolymer was importantly selected for controllable clustering of PDs to be exhibited with surficial/insulative shell, decorated with nitrogen and oxygen containing functional groups.

So, it could be concluded that, according to literature [28–33], it could be hypothesized that, chitosan-glucose conjugate as molecular precursor was exploited for clustering of PDs with excellent optical characters under both acidic and basic media via hydrothermal technique, as it advantageously exhibited with low carbonization temperature (lower than 300 °C) [34–36]. With agreement to literature, excellence of optical properties of the clustered PDs is attributed to surface passivation with nitrogen and oxygen containing groups [26–28]. In the current report, dialysis was performed eventually for ultra-purification to obtain highly purified/mono-dispersed PDs with regulated topographical properties [37].

Fig. 2 displayed FT-IR spectral mapping data of native glucose and chitosan, in addition to the spectral data of chitosan-glucose conjugate. For native glucose, the absorption bands at 3350 cm−1, 2920 cm−1, 2850 cm−1, 1450 cm−1 and 1035 cm−1are characteristic for OH stretching, asymmetric vibration of CH, symmetric vibration of CH, bending vibration of CH, vibration of CO and CC, respectively [38]. Whereas, FTIR spectra of chitosan showed characteristic bands at 3594 cm−1 (OH stretching), 2865 cm−1 (CH2 stretching), 1650–1580 cm−1 (NH bending for amine), 1174 cm−1 (COC stretching) 711 cm−1 (out-of-plane bending NH) and 564 cm−1 (out-of-plane bending NH, out-of-plane bending CO) [39]. After modification of chitosan with glucose, it could be observed that, the characteristic band o both of glucose and chitosan were retained; however, the characteristic band of NH bending became more intense, and new characteristic bands of chitosan-glucose conjugate as estimated at 1252 cm−1 (CN), 1646 cm−1 (CN), 1380 cm−1 (CN), to affirm the condensation between glucose and chitosan for successive production of the as-desired conjugate.

In 1H NMR spectrum of chitosan-glucose conjugate (Fig. 3a), the multiplet peaks at 3.70–3.73 ppm were attributed to H-1, H-2 and H-3 of chitosan nucleus; the multiplet peaks at 3.85–3.87 ppm were assigned to 2H-6, H-5, H-4 and 2-OH for chitosan moiety. [40] While multiplet peaks at 3.97–4.05 ppm were referred to 2H-6′, H-5′, H-4′ and H-3′ of glucose skeleton, multiplet peaks in the range of 5.11 to 5.16 ppm appeared owing to the OH-6′ and OH-5′ of glucose moiety, multiplet peaks at 6.50–6.54 were attributed to H-2′, OH-4′, OH-3′ and OH-2′ of glucose molecule. Additionally, The signal appeared at 8.20 ppm confirmed the presence of new proton chitosan-glucose conjugate azomethine group (which are formed by the reaction of amino group of chitosan with carbonyl group of d -glucose) and appeared as doublet with coupling constant equal 8.9 Hz (J = 8.9 Hz) due to coupling with hydrogen on the adjacent atom of glucose framework H-2′. [41]. 13C NMR spectrum of the chitosan-glucose conjugate shown in Fig. 3b, the presence of signal at 160.74 ppm spectrum attributed to carbon of new formed azomethine (CHN) group besides the presence of all the characteristic signals attributed for both glucose at and chitosan molecules. Where, signals at 102.55 ppm (C-1), 40.85 ppm (C-2), 46.85 ppm (C-3), 76.01 ppm (C-4), 54.57 ppm (C-5),and 43.36 ppm (C-6) are owing to the carbon atoms present in chitosan unit [42] and signals at 52.52 ppm (C-2′), 50.50 ppm (C-3′), 47.71 ppm (C-4′), 53.55 ppm (C-5′) and 42.34 ppm(C-6′) are referred to the carbon atoms present in glucose moieties [43]. So; it could be declared that; NMR spectra confirmed the formation of new chitosan-glucose conjugate derivative [40–42,44].

Fig. 4 represented TEM micrographs for nanostructures ingrained from glucose, chitosan and chitosan-glucose conjugate under both acidic and basic conditions after dialysis to show the topographical features of the nucleated nanostructures, whereas their size distributions were also estimated. The plotted micrographs showed, the compatibility and the essentiality for chitosan in successive clustering of spherical/highly monodispersed PDs.

From the estimated size distribution for the analyzed samples, it could be depicted that; exploitation of glucose resulted in clustering of carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), however, by functionalizing of both chitosan and chitosan-glucose conjugate PDs were produced. In addition to, regardless to the exploited molecular precursors, proceeding the synthetic route under alkaline conditions showed to be more favorable for ingrowth of smaller sized nano-structural units. These could be attributed to the effect of pH in enhancement of the accessibility and reducibility of the saccharides for clustering smaller sized nanostructures, as reported in literature [45–47].

Glucose was successfully exploited in production of CNPs under both of acidic and basic conditions; while, alkalinity was significantly affected on the size regulation to obtain smaller sized nanoparticles (14.5 ± 6.9 nm) than that nucleated under acidic conditions (28.4 ± 6.5 nm). Well surface passivation via exploitation of chitosan as covering materials to form an insulative shell with decorative functional groups is approved from the estimated data, while, the particle size was significantly diminished by functionalization of the chitosan-glucose conjugate as molecular precursor of PDs. Functionalization of chitosan under alkaline conditions resulted in growing of more controllable sized PDs (11.4 ± 4.7 nm) rather than that prepared under acidic conditions (10.6 ± 3.3 nm). Similarly, ingraining of PDs from chitosan-glucose conjugate under alkaline conditions showed to be more favorable for production of smaller sized PDs (10.6 ± 3.3 nm) compared to that clustered under acidic conditions (10.6 ± 3.3 nm). So, it could be summarized that, under alkaline conditions, the nanostructures ingrained from glucose exhibited with the biggest size (14.5 ± 6.9 nm), whereas, exploitation of chitosan (11.4 ± 4.7 nm) polymeric material resulted in ingraining of smaller sized particles, however, functionalization of chitosan-glucose conjugate showed to successively clustering the smallest PDs (5.7 ± 2.5 nm).

In contrast to any other category of nanostructures, the photoluminescence of PDs were mainly relied on well surface passivation to be capped with insulative shells with decorative groups [48]. Wu et al. confirmed that, the photoluminescence of PDs is basically correlated to the surface states and its chemical composition with decorative groups on graphitic sheets [49]. The surface states are optical centers composed via the synergetic hybridized functional groups and carbogenic cores [50]. Thus, the photoluminescent of the clustered PDs is attributed to the decorative groups clustered from chitosan with overlapping of n-π* and π-π* electronic transition for the aromatized clustered PDs. Additionally, the surface passivation mainly effects on the absorption location, leading to produce a colloidal solution of PDs with an observable color and endowing their accessible colorimetric detections. From UV–Visible absorption spectral data presented in the supplementary file, the characteristic peaks of π-π* and n-π* electronic transitions were obviously detected at 270 & 390 nm, respectively for the PDs colloids prepared under acidic conditions (G1, C1 & C3), whereas, the typical band of π-π* electronic transition was only detected at 270 nm for the PDs colloids prepared under basic conditions (G2, C2 & C4).

Herein, the photoluminescence of the clustered PDs was estimated via PL spectroscopy and the PL emission spectral results after excitation at 340 nm and were presented in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a represented the spectral mapping data for the samples G1, C1&C3 that were hydrothermally clustered under acidic conditions from glucose, chitosan and chitosan-glucose conjugate, respectively. Fig. 5b is plotted for the spectra of G2, C2&C4 nano-colloids prepared under basic conditions from glucose, chitosan and chitosan-glucose conjugate, respectively. Fig. 5c represents photo-images of the prepared samples under UV-lamp (365 nm), to show that, C1, C2 & C3 were exhibited with yellow color, while, C3 for PDs clustered from acidified chitosan is characterized with the yellow darkest color.

From the presented spectra, it could be depicted that, the estimated broad intense peaks in ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra hence affirmed the inter-composition of mobile pi-electrons of conjugated carbonic building blocks and non-bonding of hetero atoms for the ingrained PDs. Also, this demonstrates that, PL centers of PDs might be originated from the surface energy traps to become emissive as a result of surface passivation. Exhibition of strong fluorescence in visible range will consequently facilitate their application in biomedical fields and has currently elaborated by the photoluminescence spectrum of PDs.

According to literature, compared to oxygen, nitrogen doped PDs is mostly exhibited with excellent PL qualities [51]. Therefore, chitosan was selected in the current approach for harvesting PDs with long-wavelength and green emission decorated with nitrogen containing decorative groups as outstanding dopant [20]. Form the presented spectra it could be observed that, the florescence intensity was extensively higher for nano-colloids of chitosan & chitosan-glucose conjugate, while, the most intense florescence peak (7838 at wavelength of 450 nm) was observed when PDs ingrained under acidic conditions from chitosan. It could be attributed to the effect of acidity in activation and increment of chitosan accessibility for successive clustering of the as-required PDs decorated with nitrogen containing functional groups resulted in the highest PL efficiency [20]. Whereas, PDs ingrained from chitosan-glucose conjugate were observed with lower intense PL peak, attributing to conjugation between chitosan and glucose the amino-groups of chitosan might be not involved in nucleation of PDs, leading to eventual production of PDs decorated with oxygen containing decorative groups, i.e., with lower PL efficiency [25].

Fig. 6 represents FT-IR spectral results for PDs hydrothermally clustered form chitosan and chitosan-glucose conjugate under basic and acidic conditions. The plotted spectra represent the characteristic bands of PDs ingrained from chitosan and chitosan-glucose conjugate under acidic (C1 & C3) versus that prepared under basic conditions (C2 & C4), respectively. The data showed that, after conjugation of chitosan with glucose to prepare chitosan-glucose conjugate for generation of PDs under basic conditions, the characteristic peaks of chitosan were retained, however, the characteristic peaks of PDs were observably detected at 3385–3290 cm−1, 2852–2898 cm−1, 1580–1600 cm−1, 1420 cm−1 and 680–850 cm−1 corresponding to OH overlapped with NH, CH2 aliphatic, CC stretch aromatic, CN and aromatic sp2 CH bending, respectively. Moreover, by comparing between C2 & C4, the characteristic peaks of PDs were observed with higher intense. These evidently assigned for the compatibility of chitosan-glucose conjugate (C4) for generation of PDs under basic conditions with well surface passivation [52–55].

On the other hand, in Figs. 7 & 8
, NMR spectral results were plotted for approval elucidation of mechanism of PDs ingraining from C1, C3 & C4. Fig. 7 represented 1HNMR for PDs nucleated under both acidic and basic conditions. From the plotted spectral data, for the three analyzed samples, the typical peaks for protons of aliphatic amines at 1 ppm, protons attached to oxygen containing group at 4.7–5 ppm and protons of sp2 CHCH protons at 8–8.5 ppm were clearly detected. However, the as-mentioned peaks were observed to be more intense for the spectra of C4 (Fig. 7c). Fig. 8 represented 13CNMR for PDs clustered under both acidic and basic conditions. Fig. 8a represented the spectrum for nano-colloid of sample C1, as the characteristic peaks of CC aromatic or sp2 carbons was shown at 170 ppm, whereas, Fig. 8b & c for sample C4 showed typical bands at 20 ppm, 68 ppm and 183 ppm, which are corresponding to sp3 carbons, carbons attached with oxygen containing groups, and CC aromatic or sp2 carbons, respectively.

SRB assay is applicable in researching for large-scaled screening, whereas, this assay is widely beneficial for drug-toxicity examination against different normal cells and cancer cells [56]. Sulforhodamine B (SRB) assay is based on the determination of cell density, based on estimating the content cellular proteins. SRB is a bright-pink amino-xanthene dye with two sulfonic groups that under mild acidic conditions are bonded to basic amino-acid moieties, whereas, it dissociates under alkaline conditions [57]. As binding of SRB is stoichiometric, the dye extracted from stained cells contents is shown to be directly proportional to the cell masses.

The mortal action of any reagent against live cell is ascribed as the cytotoxic effects, whereas, such reagent causes the cell death via motivating of the necrosis (i.e., accidental cell death, or apoptosis, or programmed cell death). According to literature [58], apoptosis as a programmed cell-death is reported to be the most acceptable mechanism of action for the anticancer reagents. Recent researches were extensively considered with the investigation of potential reagents for motivating the apoptosis in the malignant cell. Activation of caspases is the principal step for motivation of the apoptosis. Caspase-3 is one of endoproteases i.e., an enzyme that controls the inflammation and apoptosis signal networks. Caspase-3 is also an executioner caspase that exhibit a main role in coordinating the cellular degradation, i.e., DNA fragmentation or degradation of cytoskeletal proteins [59].

From the plotted data in Fig. 9 it could be declared that, using 250 μg/mL from the prepared PDs colloids resulted in significant diminishing in the cell viability percent to be 54.4 % & 61.4 % for C1 & C2 (Fig. 9a) and 60.3 % & 44.7 % for C3 & C4 (Fig. 9b), respectively. Fig. 9c showed the relation between the concentration of the applied samples as antitumor reagents and the estimated values of IC50, while, the order for affinity of the prepared samples as antiviral laborer with decrement of IC50 follows order; C2 < C1 ≤ C3 < C4. From the represented data, the estimated IC50 values for C1, C2, C3 and C4 were 287.5, 356.5, 282.4, and 165.4 μg/mL, respectively.

In agreement to the currently investigated anticancer potency of the prepared samples, it could be approved that, 1000 μg/mL of PDs colloid ingrained from the currently prepared chitosan-glucose conjugate could be promisingly applicable as a new platform for anticancer laborers with mortality percent of 82.7–87.2 %. These findings significantly confirmed that; the PDs that were currently prepared in the presented approach were potentially exhibited significant mortal effects against human non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) cell line, A549. However, PDs nucleated from either chitosan-glucose conjugate showed higher affinity for cytotoxic action and anti-cancer performance against the as-tested lung cancer cell line rather than that synthesized from chitosan. The synthesized PDs from chitosan-glucose conjugate in alkaline medium exhibited the highest anticancer activity.

According to literature [60], the anticancer performance and anti-proliferative action of the prepared PDs in the current work could illustrated as, exposing to PDs was mainly resulted in activating of caspase-3 of lung cancer cell lines, and consequently leading to apoptotic cell death, as the nucleated PDs with structure containing free mobile electrons and it could inhibit the growth of the as-examined lung cancer cells by generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for activation of caspase-3, that could act directly in apoptosis. It could be concluded that, the synthesized PDs in the current approach could be described as superiorly bio-applicable anticancer candidates. Moreover, the data approved that the modification of chitosan through Maillard reaction improved the anticancer activity of the obtained PDs.

Different diseases that are caused by viral infection could dramatically lead to eventual death. Treatment for most of these diseases became extensively considered as a researching point especially if the viral cells are capable to modify for being resistant against different drugs that unfortunately results in more expensive medical costing and increasing the death rate. Few reports were recently considered with investigating of the potency of carbon-based nanomaterials for microbicidal activity and/or diminishing the infectious effect. Du et al., were studied that carbon-based nanomaterials could excellently characterized with viricidal action for pseudo rabies virus as example for DNA virus and porcine reproductive respiratory syndrome virus as a type of RNA virus via inhibiting their reproductive activities [61]. Type I interferons (IFNs) as two main categories of IFN-α and IFN-β, are known as the best instinctive antiviral immune reagents with excellent antiviral affinity for treatment of human beings [62].

Therefore, it was suggested that, the antiviral effects of carbon-based nanomaterials was attributed to the induction for producing ISGs (Interferon Simulated Genes) and activating IFN-α, that consequently leads to inhibit the viral multiplication [61]. Another study reported that, carbon based nanomaterials can be applicable as viricidal reagents via inducing of apoptosis, that was mainly mediated via generating of reactive oxygen species (ROS), motivating the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria, activating of caspase3, cleaving of phospholipase C-γ1, and eventually results in deterioration of DNA moieties [63].

A comparative study between the antiviral performances of the prepared PDs samples (C1, C2, C3 & C4) against Low Pathogenic Corona Virus (229E) was currently studied. In the presented study, the CPE-inhibition assay was performed to identify the potential antiviral action against Low Pathogenic Corona Virus (229E). The antiviral potency of the prepared colloids is evaluated via the optical activity. Additionally, the dose-response assay was carried out in order to decide the range of affinity for the prepared nano-colloids, i.e., the 50 % inhibitory concentration (IC50), as well as the range of cytotoxicity (CC50). The infectivity of Low Pathogenic Corona Virus (229E) was detected via the crystal violet methodology, which monitored CPE to calculate the cell viability percent. The relation between the concentration of the prepared nano-colloids and the optical density & cell viability percent was shown in Fig. 10 . From the presented figures, it could be depicted that, the optical density was gradually decreased with increment the dose/concentration of the prepared colloids. For more confirmation, the cell viability percent was shown to be gradually decreased with increment log value of the concentration (μg/mL).

The data in Fig. 10 declared that, using 250 mg/mL from the synthesized PDs exhibited the cell viability percentage of 69.7, 33.5, 48.9 and 17.1 % for C1, C2, C3 and C4, respectively. Using 1000 mg/mL from any of the prepared colloids resulted in full reduction in the cell viability (Fig. 10a&b). Fig. 10c showed the relation between the concentration of the applied samples as antiviral reagents and the estimated values of IC50. From the represented data, the obtained IC50 for C1, C2, C3 and C4 was 147.4, 29.6, 43.6 and 19.3 mg/mL, respectively. Whereas, the CC50 values for C1, C2, C3 and C4 were 222.2, 98.9, 149.0, and 49.5 mg/mL were respectively.

Eventually, compared to the PDs obtained from chitosan, the synthesized PDs from chitosan-glucose conjugate showed higher antiviral activity especially those prepared in alkaline medium. Whereas, the Maillard reaction products is well-known to progress efficiently as bioactive reagents [64]. The modification of chitosan through Maillard reaction is resulted in obtaining PDs with higher antiviral activity. Hence, the PDs clustered under basic conditions from chitosan-glucose conjugate is exhibited with the highest affinity rather than chitosan to be concurrently applicable as anticancer/antiviral laborer.

The current study focused on the effect of Maillard reaction for conjugation of chitosan with glucose for enhancement the affinity of chitosan as a biological resource of PDs. The affinity of glucose & chitosan versus chitosan-glucose conjugate as molecular precursors for hydrothermal nucleation of PDs to be concurrently applicable as antiviral and anticancer laborers. Successive nucleation of CNPs and PDs by exploitation of glucose, chitosan and chitosan-glucose conjugate, was affirmed via several instrumental characterizations including TEM, FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. Subsequently, the photoluminescent properties of the prepared CNPs & PDs were monitored. PDs were nucleated from chitosan (C1 acidic, 10.6 nm & C2 basic, 11.4 nm) and from chitosan-glucose conjugate (C3 acidic, 6.8 nm & C4 basic, 5.7 nm). The estimated IC50 values against the lung cancer cell were 282.4 and 165.4 μg/mL in case of PDs (C3 and C4), respectively. While the obtained IC50 against corona virus for PDs (C3 and C4) was 43.6 and 19.3 mg/mL, respectively. The obtained data approved that the Maillard reaction effectively enhanced the biological affinity of chitosan for ingraining PDs to be applicable as antiviral and antitumor laborers. The current study will open the way for further modification of polysaccharides to enhance their uses and applications.
Authors are equally contributed in the current work.
The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2022.10.172.
